title: CDMGNotation
lean_code_with_comments:
instance : Membership α (CDMG α) where
  mem G v := v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V

/-- LN's `\tuh`: there is a *directed* edge `v₁ → v₂` in `G`, i.e.
`(v₁, v₂) ∈ G.E`. -/
def tuh (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := (v₁, v₂) ∈ G.E

/-- LN's `\hut`: there is a *directed* edge `v₁ ← v₂` in `G`, i.e.
`(v₂, v₁) ∈ G.E` (the same `G.E` membership, with arguments swapped). -/
def hut (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := (v₂, v₁) ∈ G.E

/-- LN's `\huh`: there is a *bidirected* edge `v₁ ↔ v₂` in `G`, i.e.
`(v₁, v₂) ∈ G.L`. Because `G.L` is required to be symmetric (see
`CDMG.L_symm` in `def_3_1`), `huh G v₁ v₂` and `huh G v₂ v₁` are
propositionally equivalent. -/
def huh (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := (v₁, v₂) ∈ G.L

/-- LN's `\suh`: there is an edge with an arrowhead at `v₂` in `G`,
i.e. either `tuh G v₁ v₂` (`→`) or `huh G v₁ v₂` (`↔`). -/
def suh (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := tuh G v₁ v₂ ∨ huh G v₁ v₂

/-- LN's `\hus`: there is an edge with an arrowhead at `v₁` in `G`,
i.e. either `hut G v₁ v₂` (`←`) or `huh G v₁ v₂` (`↔`). -/
def hus (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := hut G v₁ v₂ ∨ huh G v₁ v₂

/-- LN's `\sus`: there is *some* edge between `v₁` and `v₂` in `G`,
i.e. any of `tuh G v₁ v₂`, `hut G v₁ v₂`, or `huh G v₁ v₂`. This is
the "adjacency" relation used in def 3.3. -/
def sus (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop :=
  tuh G v₁ v₂ ∨ hut G v₁ v₂ ∨ huh G v₁ v₂

/-- LN `v₁ ⟶[G] v₂`: directed edge `v₁ → v₂` in the CDMG `G`. Matches
LN macro `\tuh`. Unicode arrow is `⟶` (`\longrightarrow`, U+27F6),
chosen distinct from the function arrow `→` to avoid any clash with
Lean's built-in syntax. -/
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⟶[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.tuh G v₁ v₂

/-- LN `v₁ ⟵[G] v₂`: directed edge `v₁ ← v₂` in `G`. Matches LN macro
`\hut`. Equivalent to `v₂ ⟶[G] v₁` by definition. -/
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⟵[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.hut G v₁ v₂

/-- LN `v₁ ⟷[G] v₂`: bidirected edge `v₁ ↔ v₂` in `G`. Matches LN
macro `\huh`. Symmetric in `v₁`/`v₂` thanks to `CDMG.L_symm`. -/
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⟷[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.huh G v₁ v₂

/-- LN `v₁ ⇸[G] v₂`: there is an arrowhead at `v₂` (`→` or `↔`).
Matches LN macro `\suh`. Unicode is `⇸` (U+21F8, RIGHTWARDS ARROW
WITH VERTICAL STROKE) -- the vertical stroke evokes the LN's star
placeholder at the left endpoint. -/
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⇸[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.suh G v₁ v₂

/-- LN `v₁ ⇷[G] v₂`: there is an arrowhead at `v₁` (`←` or `↔`).
Matches LN macro `\hus`. Unicode is `⇷` (U+21F7, LEFTWARDS ARROW
WITH VERTICAL STROKE). -/
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⇷[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.hus G v₁ v₂

/-- LN `v₁ ↮[G] v₂`: any edge of any orientation between `v₁` and
`v₂` ("adjacency"). Matches LN macro `\sus`. Unicode is `↮` (U+21AE,
LEFT RIGHT ARROW WITH STROKE) -- the doubled stroke evokes a
placeholder at both endpoints. -/
scoped notation:50 v₁ " ↮[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.sus G v₁ v₂
lean_code_without_comments:
instance : Membership α (CDMG α) where
  mem G v := v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V

def tuh (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := (v₁, v₂) ∈ G.E

def hut (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := (v₂, v₁) ∈ G.E

def huh (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := (v₁, v₂) ∈ G.L

def suh (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := tuh G v₁ v₂ ∨ huh G v₁ v₂

def hus (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := hut G v₁ v₂ ∨ huh G v₁ v₂

def sus (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop :=
  tuh G v₁ v₂ ∨ hut G v₁ v₂ ∨ huh G v₁ v₂

scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⟶[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.tuh G v₁ v₂

scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⟵[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.hut G v₁ v₂

scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⟷[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.huh G v₁ v₂

scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⇸[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.suh G v₁ v₂

scoped notation:50 v₁ " ⇷[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.hus G v₁ v₂

scoped notation:50 v₁ " ↮[" G "] " v₂ => Causality.CDMG.sus G v₁ v₂
lean_explanation:
This file equips the `CDMG α` structure from def 3.1 with the seven pieces of notation introduced in the LN's `\begin{Not}` block. As in def 3.1, `α : Type*` is the ambient vertex carrier; nothing about `α` is assumed, matching the LN's silent treatment of the vertex set as an arbitrary collection. The fixed argument `G : CDMG α` is the graph being discussed, and `v`, `v₁`, `v₂ : α` are arbitrary vertices in that carrier.

The first item registers a `Membership` instance, the Mathlib typeclass that gives the literal Lean syntax `v ∈ G` its meaning. Unfolding to `v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V`, it states that "being in `G`" means being either an input node $v \in J$ or an output node $v \in V$. The companion `simp` lemma `CDMG.mem_iff` exposes that unfolding to the rewriter, so any later proof can dispatch `v ∈ G` into the standard `Set.mem_union` machinery.

The next three definitions (`tuh`, `hut`, `huh`) encode the three primitive edge predicates: a directed edge $v_1 \to v_2$, its reverse, and a bidirected edge. Each is a one-line `Prop`-valued definition that reads off membership in `G.E` (for directed) or `G.L` (for bidirected). The names mirror the LN's TeX macros `\tuh`, `\hut`, `\huh` ("tail-at-`v₁` head-at-`v₂`" and similar), so a reader bouncing between the notes and the Lean source can trace the correspondence directly.

The "star" relations `suh`, `hus`, `sus` encode the LN's wildcard "arrowhead-or-tail" notation: `suh` asks for an arrowhead at $v_2$ (i.e. $\to$ or $\leftrightarrow$), `hus` for an arrowhead at $v_1$, and `sus` for *any* edge in either orientation. They are spelled out as disjunctions over the three primitive predicates, which matches how the LN reasons about them.

Finally, six `scoped notation:50` declarations bind Unicode arrow tokens — `⟶[G]`, `⟵[G]`, `⟷[G]`, `⇸[G]`, `⇷[G]`, `↮[G]` — to those predicates. The notations live in the `Causality.CDMG` namespace; downstream files unlock them with `open scoped Causality.CDMG`. The `[G]`-bracketed suffix makes the ambient graph explicit at the syntactic layer, mirroring the LN's "$\in G$" postfix without overloading Lean's own `∈`.
design_choices:
**`Membership` instance rather than a bespoke `CDMG.mem` predicate.** The LN consistently writes the phrase $v \in G$, so we register a `Membership` instance that lets Lean accept that exact syntax. The alternative — a predicate `CDMG.mem G v` — would force `G.mem v` (or its dot-notation cousin) at every call site and would miss out on Mathlib's `Set.mem_union`, `Set.mem_insert_iff` and related rewrite lemmas. Registering the typeclass gives us the LN phrasing for free and routes membership reasoning through the standard library.

**Six separate `def`s for the edge relations rather than a single `inductive EdgeKind`.** One tempting alternative is an enum with constructors for directed, reverse, and bidirected edges, together with a single `hasEdge : EdgeKind → CDMG α → α → α → Prop`. We rejected this for two reasons. First, the LN treats each phrase such as $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ as an *atom*: later definitions use $\{w \mid w \tuh v \in G\}$ as a set comprehension and other results name $j \hus v \in G$ as a forbidden pattern. Atomic predicates compose more naturally with `simp`, `rw`, and pattern matching than a wrapped enum-indexed predicate. Second, the three "star" relations are *disjunctions*, not single edge kinds; an enum would not capture them, so we would have ended up writing disjunctions anyway. Spelling them out directly mirrors the LN's own approach.

**Unicode notation choices.** We deliberately avoid the short arrows `→`, `←`, `↔`, which are heavily overloaded in Lean (function arrows, `Iff`, the rewrite-direction marker, and so on). The long arrows `⟶`, `⟵`, `⟷` (U+27F6/F5/F7) carry the LN's macros `\tuh` / `\hut` / `\huh` without colliding with built-in syntax. The "star" relations use `⇸`, `⇷`, `↮`: the vertical strokes (U+21F8 and U+21F7) and the doubled stroke of U+21AE evoke the LN's "star = arrowhead-or-tail" wildcard, with one or two endpoints respectively.

**`Prop`-valued, not `Bool`-valued.** `Prop` matches the LN's mathematical phrasing and lets us write `(v₁, v₂) ∈ G.E` directly (set membership is already `Prop`-valued). A `Bool` encoding would force `DecidableEq α` on the vertex type, which the LN explicitly does not assume — later chapters use real-valued vertices, where decidable equality is unavailable. Where decidability does matter later (e.g. constructive algorithms over finite graphs) it can be introduced as a separate hypothesis.

**Scoped rather than global notation.** Binding the arrows with `scoped` keeps the Unicode tokens inside the `Causality.CDMG` namespace. A file that has no business with CDMGs does not pay for them, and downstream rows opt in with `open scoped Causality.CDMG`. The `[G]` suffix (rather than an `$\in G$` postfix) makes the ambient graph explicit at the notation layer and sidesteps any temptation to overload Lean's own `∈`.
